lines.line: # Introduction and Consent

> The interview begins with Dr. Fiona Vernal introducing herself and the purpose of the oral history project. She requests verbal consent from Mr. Samuel Smith Jr., who provides his name, address, and permission to record the interview. The interviewer then asks Mr. Smith to begin by sharing his family history, setting the stage for the conversation.

metadata.title: Introduction and Consent
metadata.synopsis: The interview begins with Dr. Fiona Vernal introducing herself and the purpose of the oral history project. She requests verbal consent from Mr. Samuel Smith Jr., who provides his name, address, and permission to record the interview. The interviewer then asks Mr. Smith to begin by sharing his family history, setting the stage for the conversation.

lines.line: # Family Roots and Early Life in Alabama

> The speaker traces his family lineage back to the early 1900s, describing his grandfather and father’s experiences as sharecroppers in Marion Junction, Alabama. He recounts his father’s decision to leave sharecropping and move the family to Selma, Alabama, in search of a better life. The speaker describes the black-owned businesses and self-sufficiency of the black community in Selma, as well as the realities of segregation and how it shaped their daily lives.

metadata.title: Family Roots and Early Life in Alabama
metadata.synopsis: The speaker traces his family lineage back to the early 1900s, describing his grandfather and father’s experiences as sharecroppers in Marion Junction, Alabama. He recounts his father’s decision to leave sharecropping and move the family to Selma, Alabama, in search of a better life. The speaker describes the black-owned businesses and self-sufficiency of the black community in Selma, as well as the realities of segregation and how it shaped their daily lives.

lines.line: # Schooling and Segregation in Selma

> The speaker discusses his educational journey in Selma, attending several schools and participating in sports. He reflects on his experiences with segregation, noting that while the city was divided along racial lines, he and his peers felt comfortable within their own community. He describes the boundaries set by parents and the lack of direct conflict with white residents during his youth.

metadata.title: Schooling and Segregation in Selma
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses his educational journey in Selma, attending several schools and participating in sports. He reflects on his experiences with segregation, noting that while the city was divided along racial lines, he and his peers felt comfortable within their own community. He describes the boundaries set by parents and the lack of direct conflict with white residents during his youth.

lines.line: # Migration North and New Beginnings in Connecticut

> At age 17, the speaker leaves Selma for Connecticut, seeking better opportunities as part of the broader migration of young black southerners to the North. He describes his arrival in Hartford, the welcoming environment, and the presence of other migrants from Selma. He quickly finds work at Way Best Chicken Market and begins to establish himself in his new home.

metadata.title: Migration North and New Beginnings in Connecticut
metadata.synopsis: At age 17, the speaker leaves Selma for Connecticut, seeking better opportunities as part of the broader migration of young black southerners to the North. He describes his arrival in Hartford, the welcoming environment, and the presence of other migrants from Selma. He quickly finds work at Way Best Chicken Market and begins to establish himself in his new home.

lines.line: # Meeting Rosalind and Early Married Life

> The speaker recounts meeting his future wife, Rosalind, at age 19 and the emotional impact of his mother's early death. He describes their courtship, marriage, and the early years of their life together, including moving to Stow Village and his continued employment. The narrative touches on his search for emotional healing and the importance of finding a supportive partner.

metadata.title: Meeting Rosalind and Early Married Life
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recounts meeting his future wife, Rosalind, at age 19 and the emotional impact of his mother's early death. He describes their courtship, marriage, and the early years of their life together, including moving to Stow Village and his continued employment. The narrative touches on his search for emotional healing and the importance of finding a supportive partner.

lines.line: # Marion Junction: Family Roots and Father's Background

> Prompted by the interviewer, the speaker provides more detail about Marion Junction, describing it as a tiny, rural community with minimal infrastructure. He explains his father's background as a sharecropper, his family's ties to the area, and the migration patterns of his father's siblings. The speaker also discusses his father's feelings of inferiority and his efforts to find work and develop skills outside the family business.

metadata.title: Marion Junction: Family Roots and Father's Background
metadata.synopsis: Prompted by the interviewer, the speaker provides more detail about Marion Junction, describing it as a tiny, rural community with minimal infrastructure. He explains his father's background as a sharecropper, his family's ties to the area, and the migration patterns of his father's siblings. The speaker also discusses his father's feelings of inferiority and his efforts to find work and develop skills outside the family business.

lines.line: # Teaching His Father to Read and Family Dynamics

> The speaker describes the process of teaching his illiterate father to read and write, emphasizing the importance his father placed on education and financial literacy. He shares how he read the Bible and newspaper to his father, eventually helping him learn to read and sign his name. The speaker also outlines the structure of his blended family and the family's history of working on a white-owned farm in Marion Junction.

metadata.title: Teaching His Father to Read and Family Dynamics
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes the process of teaching his illiterate father to read and write, emphasizing the importance his father placed on education and financial literacy. He shares how he read the Bible and newspaper to his father, eventually helping him learn to read and sign his name. The speaker also outlines the structure of his blended family and the family's history of working on a white-owned farm in Marion Junction.

lines.line: # Memories of Selma and Cotton Picking

> The speaker reflects fondly on his childhood in Selma, noting that he did not experience the racial violence others reported. He describes working in the cotton fields as a child, the low pay, and the exploitative credit system that often left workers in debt. Despite the hardships, he recalls the camaraderie and fun he had with other children while working in the fields.

metadata.title: Memories of Selma and Cotton Picking
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects fondly on his childhood in Selma, noting that he did not experience the racial violence others reported. He describes working in the cotton fields as a child, the low pay, and the exploitative credit system that often left workers in debt. Despite the hardships, he recalls the camaraderie and fun he had with other children while working in the fields.

lines.line: # Family Migration, Ambitions, and Self-Esteem

> The speaker discusses the migration of his uncles to cities like Atlanta and Detroit, their business successes, and his own ambitions inspired by civics class. He describes his desire to see the world, his initial plan to go to Rhode Island, and the factors that led him to Connecticut. The speaker also touches on his struggles with self-esteem and the influence of his upbringing on his aspirations.

metadata.title: Family Migration, Ambitions, and Self-Esteem
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the migration of his uncles to cities like Atlanta and Detroit, their business successes, and his own ambitions inspired by civics class. He describes his desire to see the world, his initial plan to go to Rhode Island, and the factors that led him to Connecticut. The speaker also touches on his struggles with self-esteem and the influence of his upbringing on his aspirations.

lines.line: # Tobacco Work, Arrival in Hartford, and Social Networks

> The speaker explains the seasonal migration of young men from Selma to Connecticut for tobacco work, the stories they brought back, and how these influenced his decision to move north. He describes the process of finding work and housing in Hartford, the support provided by friends, and the vibrant social life among southern migrants, including the concept of 'homeboys' and the importance of community connections.

metadata.title: Tobacco Work, Arrival in Hartford, and Social Networks
metadata.synopsis: The speaker explains the seasonal migration of young men from Selma to Connecticut for tobacco work, the stories they brought back, and how these influenced his decision to move north. He describes the process of finding work and housing in Hartford, the support provided by friends, and the vibrant social life among southern migrants, including the concept of 'homeboys' and the importance of community connections.

lines.line: # Early Work Experiences and Union Involvement

> The speaker confirms the positive reports about Hartford, describing the abundance of jobs and his early employment at Way Best Chicken Market. He discusses his initial attempts to get involved in union activities, his move to KF Brick Company, and the challenges he faced as a young black man with low self-esteem. The narrative highlights his growing interest in labor organizing and the desire to help others through union work.

metadata.title: Early Work Experiences and Union Involvement
metadata.synopsis: The speaker confirms the positive reports about Hartford, describing the abundance of jobs and his early employment at Way Best Chicken Market. He discusses his initial attempts to get involved in union activities, his move to KF Brick Company, and the challenges he faced as a young black man with low self-esteem. The narrative highlights his growing interest in labor organizing and the desire to help others through union work.

lines.line: # Union Leadership, Mentorship, and Achievements

> The speaker details his rise in union leadership, mentored by Tony Santo, an Italian union representative. He describes learning the intricacies of union contracts, becoming a chief shop steward, and advocating for workers' rights. His accomplishments include introducing the 401K and HMOs, improving vacation and retirement benefits, and fostering collaboration between black and white workers. The speaker credits his wife and his network for providing valuable information and support.

metadata.title: Union Leadership, Mentorship, and Achievements
metadata.synopsis: The speaker details his rise in union leadership, mentored by Tony Santo, an Italian union representative. He describes learning the intricacies of union contracts, becoming a chief shop steward, and advocating for workers' rights. His accomplishments include introducing the 401K and HMOs, improving vacation and retirement benefits, and fostering collaboration between black and white workers. The speaker credits his wife and his network for providing valuable information and support.

lines.line: # Family Life, Housing, and Community in Hartford

> The speaker recounts his marriage to Rosalind, the growth of their family, and their moves within Hartford, including living in Stow Village and purchasing a home on Adams Street. He describes Hartford as a thriving, welcoming city with ample opportunities and a strong sense of community. The narrative touches on the importance of stable employment and the support provided by the local black community.

metadata.title: Family Life, Housing, and Community in Hartford
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recounts his marriage to Rosalind, the growth of their family, and their moves within Hartford, including living in Stow Village and purchasing a home on Adams Street. He describes Hartford as a thriving, welcoming city with ample opportunities and a strong sense of community. The narrative touches on the importance of stable employment and the support provided by the local black community.

lines.line: # Settling in Hartford: Social and Daily Life

> Upon arriving in Hartford, the speaker relied on friends for temporary housing and quickly found work. He describes the system of renting rooms, eating at George and Mary's Restaurant, and the social networks that helped newcomers adjust. The speaker also discusses recreational activities, such as dances and sports, and the process of integrating into the local youth community.

metadata.title: Settling in Hartford: Social and Daily Life
metadata.synopsis: Upon arriving in Hartford, the speaker relied on friends for temporary housing and quickly found work. He describes the system of renting rooms, eating at George and Mary's Restaurant, and the social networks that helped newcomers adjust. The speaker also discusses recreational activities, such as dances and sports, and the process of integrating into the local youth community.

lines.line: # Religious Conversion and Spiritual Journey

> The speaker shares his spiritual awakening, which began during a visit to Selma and culminated in his conversion at Saint John Full Gospel Church in Hartford. He describes the profound impact of hearing about the love of God, his struggle to embrace change, and the transformative experience of accepting Christianity. The narrative highlights the role of faith in providing direction and purpose in his life.

metadata.title: Religious Conversion and Spiritual Journey
metadata.synopsis: The speaker shares his spiritual awakening, which began during a visit to Selma and culminated in his conversion at Saint John Full Gospel Church in Hartford. He describes the profound impact of hearing about the love of God, his struggle to embrace change, and the transformative experience of accepting Christianity. The narrative highlights the role of faith in providing direction and purpose in his life.

lines.line: # Church Leadership and Vision

> The speaker discusses his desire to be active in church, eventually becoming Sunday school superintendent and youth leader at Saint John’s. He reflects on the influence of his childhood experiences and the parallels between union leadership and church work. The speaker emphasizes the importance of vision, collaboration, and mentorship in building successful ministries and describes his two decades of service at Saint John’s.

metadata.title: Church Leadership and Vision
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses his desire to be active in church, eventually becoming Sunday school superintendent and youth leader at Saint John’s. He reflects on the influence of his childhood experiences and the parallels between union leadership and church work. The speaker emphasizes the importance of vision, collaboration, and mentorship in building successful ministries and describes his two decades of service at Saint John’s.

lines.line: # Move to Enfield and Founding a Ministry

> Facing a sense of calling, the speaker describes the difficult decision to leave Hartford and Saint John’s Church for Enfield. He recounts the process of buying a house, the challenges of relocating, and the realization that his ministry would focus on helping alcoholics, drug addicts, and the marginalized. The speaker details the founding of his ministry in his home, the outreach to those in need, and the unique challenges of serving in a predominantly white community.

metadata.title: Move to Enfield and Founding a Ministry
metadata.synopsis: Facing a sense of calling, the speaker describes the difficult decision to leave Hartford and Saint John’s Church for Enfield. He recounts the process of buying a house, the challenges of relocating, and the realization that his ministry would focus on helping alcoholics, drug addicts, and the marginalized. The speaker details the founding of his ministry in his home, the outreach to those in need, and the unique challenges of serving in a predominantly white community.

lines.line: # Changes in Migration and Hartford’s Demographics

> The speaker discusses the end of the Great Migration, as economic opportunities in the South improved and fewer people moved north. He describes the demographic shifts in Hartford, including the movement of black and Jewish populations, and the role of government programs like Project Matthew in facilitating black homeownership. The narrative highlights the evolving nature of community and the impact of broader social and economic trends.

metadata.title: Changes in Migration and Hartford’s Demographics
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the end of the Great Migration, as economic opportunities in the South improved and fewer people moved north. He describes the demographic shifts in Hartford, including the movement of black and Jewish populations, and the role of government programs like Project Matthew in facilitating black homeownership. The narrative highlights the evolving nature of community and the impact of broader social and economic trends.

lines.line: # Black Community in Enfield and Social Dynamics

> The speaker reflects on the absence of a cohesive black community in Enfield, noting that black residents are scattered and often do not know or support each other. He contrasts this with the strong sense of community he experienced in Hartford and attributes the lack of connection in Enfield to factors such as transportation, social preferences, and the challenges of building new networks in a predominantly white area.

metadata.title: Black Community in Enfield and Social Dynamics
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the absence of a cohesive black community in Enfield, noting that black residents are scattered and often do not know or support each other. He contrasts this with the strong sense of community he experienced in Hartford and attributes the lack of connection in Enfield to factors such as transportation, social preferences, and the challenges of building new networks in a predominantly white area.

lines.line: # Reflections on Enfield, Black Identity, and Community

> The speaker considers the challenges of living as a black person in a majority-white city, noting that while he personally adapted due to his diverse experiences, many black residents in Enfield remain isolated. He observes that new arrivals often do not connect with others, and that the black community in Enfield lacks the cohesion and mutual support found in other places. The speaker speculates on the reasons for this, including social preferences and the effects of suburban living.

metadata.title: Reflections on Enfield, Black Identity, and Community
metadata.synopsis: The speaker considers the challenges of living as a black person in a majority-white city, noting that while he personally adapted due to his diverse experiences, many black residents in Enfield remain isolated. He observes that new arrivals often do not connect with others, and that the black community in Enfield lacks the cohesion and mutual support found in other places. The speaker speculates on the reasons for this, including social preferences and the effects of suburban living.

lines.line: # Reflections on Hartford and Selma Today

> The speaker shares his observations on the changes in Hartford, noting the decline of familiar neighborhoods and the shifting patterns of community life. He also discusses his ongoing connection to Selma, Alabama, expressing concern for the city’s well-being after a recent tornado and his continued visits to his hometown. The narrative underscores the enduring importance of place and memory in the speaker’s life.

metadata.title: Reflections on Hartford and Selma Today
metadata.synopsis: The speaker shares his observations on the changes in Hartford, noting the decline of familiar neighborhoods and the shifting patterns of community life. He also discusses his ongoing connection to Selma, Alabama, expressing concern for the city’s well-being after a recent tornado and his continued visits to his hometown. The narrative underscores the enduring importance of place and memory in the speaker’s life.

lines.line: # Closing, Project Information, and Final Reflections

> The interview concludes with a discussion of the oral history project’s goals, the fate of the brick company where the speaker worked, and the impact of his union advocacy. The speaker reflects on his motivation to help others, particularly in securing better health benefits for coworkers. The conversation ends with expressions of gratitude and plans for future collaboration on the project.

story.title: Enfield Oral History Interview Link
story.description: The transcript is an oral history interview with Samuel Swift Jr., conducted by Dr. Fiona Vernal. It covers Swift's family history, beginning with his sharecropper father in early 20th-century Alabama. Swift recounts his childhood experiences with segregation in Selma, Alabama, and his move to Connecticut in 1957 for better opportunities. He discusses his work at Waybest Chicken Market and KF Brick and his involvement in union leadership. Swift also describes a significant spiritual awakening leading to his dedication to ministry work. He provides insights into community dynamics in Enfield, highlighting challenges in forming a cohesive Black community.
